title: Registers and Basic ARM Instructions
summary: ARM has 16 general-purpose 32-bit registers (R0-R15). This lesson covers the core instructions: MOV, ADD, SUB, AND, ORR, LSL, LSR, and how to write simple ARM programs.
content_html:
ARM Registers

ARM has 16 general-purpose 32-bit registers in 32-bit mode:

RegisterNamePurpose
R0-R12General purposeAvailable for any use
R13SP (Stack Pointer)Points to top of stack
R14LR (Link Register)Stores return address for function calls
R15PC (Program Counter)Address of next instruction to execute

FASMARM / ARMlite Syntax

; Comments start with semicolon LABEL = value ; define a constant label: ; mark a memory address (ends with colon) ; #decimal → decimal literal ; $hex → hex literal (FASMARM) / 0x in ARMlite

Core Instructions

InstructionSyntaxDescriptionExample
MOVMOV Rd, operandLoad value into registerMOV R0, #5 → R0=5
ADDADD Rd, Rn, operandRd = Rn + operandADD R0, R0, #1 → R0++
SUBSUB Rd, Rn, operandRd = Rn - operandSUB R1, R1, #3
ANDAND Rd, Rn, operandBitwise ANDAND R0, R0, #0xFF
ORRORR Rd, Rn, operandBitwise ORORR R0, R0, #0x10
LSLLSL Rd, Rn, #nLogical shift left (×2ⁿ)LSL R1, R1, #3 → R1×8
LSRLSR Rd, Rn, #nLogical shift right (÷2ⁿ)LSR R1, R1, #1 → R1÷2

Example Program: Turn on an LED (bare-metal RPi)

BASE = $FE000000 ; GPIO base address (RPi4) GPIO_OFFSET = $200000 ; offset to GPIO registers mov r0, BASE orr r0, GPIO_OFFSET ; r0 = 0xFE200000 mov r1, #1 lsl r1, #24 ; shift 1 to bit 24 str r1, [r0, #4] ; enable GPIO18 as output mov r1, #1 lsl r1, #18 ; shift 1 to bit 18 str r1, [r0, #28] ; set GPIO18 high (LED on) loop$: b loop$ ; loop forever
LSL and multiply by 2: Shifting left by n bits is equivalent to multiplying by 2ⁿ. LSL R1, #3 = R1 × 8. This is how GPIO register offsets are calculated.
MOV limitation: MOV cannot directly load all 32-bit numbers. For large constants, use LDR with a literal pool.
